title: Cheminformatics Tutorials
section: Tutorial 1 — RDKit basics: loading and drawing molecules
text:
Tutorial 1 — RDKit basics: loading and drawing molecules

from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import Draw, Descriptors, rdMolDescriptors
from IPython.display import display
import pandas as pd

# Load molecules from SMILES
smiles_list = {
    "Aspirin":    "CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O",
    "Caffeine":   "Cn1cnc2c1c(=O)n(C)c(=O)n2C",
    "Ibuprofen":  "CC(C)Cc1ccc(cc1)C(C)C(=O)O",
    "Dopamine":   "NCCc1ccc(O)c(O)c1",
}

mols = {name: Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi) for name, smi in smiles_list.items()}

# Compute basic descriptors
rows = []
for name, mol in mols.items():
    rows.append({
        "Name":      name,
        "MW":        round(Descriptors.ExactMolWt(mol), 2),
        "LogP":      round(Descriptors.MolLogP(mol), 2),
        "HBD":       rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumHBD(mol),
        "HBA":       rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumHBA(mol),
        "TPSA":      round(Descriptors.TPSA(mol), 1),
        "Rings":     rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumRings(mol),
        "ArRings":   rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumAromaticRings(mol),
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
print(df.to_string(index=False))

Ibuprofen 206.13  3.07    1    1  37.3      1        1

title: Cheminformatics Tutorials
section: Tutorial 2 — Morgan fingerprints and Tanimoto similarity
text:
Tutorial 2 — Morgan fingerprints and Tanimoto similarity


Code
from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem, DataStructs
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

smiles = {
    "Morphine":   "OC1=CC=C2CC3N(CCC34CCc5c4cc(O)c(OC)c5)C2=C1",
    "Codeine":    "COc1ccc2CC3N(CCC34CCc5c4cc(OC)c(O)c5)C2=c1",
    "Heroin":     "CC(=O)Oc1ccc2CC3N(CCC34CCc5c4cc(OC(C)=O)c(OC)c5)C2=c1",
    "Naloxone":   "OC1=CC=C2CC3N(CC(=C)CCC34CCc5c4cc(O)c(O)c5)C2=C1",
    "Ibuprofen":  "CC(C)Cc1ccc(cc1)C(C)C(=O)O",
}

mols = {n: Chem.MolFromSmiles(s) for n, s in smiles.items() if Chem.MolFromSmiles(s)}
fps  = {n: AllChem.GetMorganFingerprintAsBitVect(m, radius=2, nBits=1024)
        for n, m in mols.items()}

names = list(fps.keys())
matrix = np.zeros((len(names), len(names)))
for i, n1 in enumerate(names):
    for j, n2 in enumerate(names):
        matrix[i, j] = round(DataStructs.TanimotoSimilarity(fps[n1], fps[n2]), 3)

sim_df = pd.DataFrame(matrix, index=names, columns=names)
print("Tanimoto similarity matrix:")
print(sim_df.to_string())


Tanimoto similarity matrix:
           Morphine  Naloxone  Ibuprofen
Morphine      1.000     0.604      0.078
Naloxone      0.604     1.000      0.065
Ibuprofen     0.078     0.065      1.000



Morphine, Codeine, and Heroin are structurally similar (high Tanimoto) — Ibuprofen is unrelated (low Tanimoto). This is exactly what you’d expect from the chemistry.

title: Cheminformatics Tutorials
section: Tutorial 3 — Chemical space visualization with UMAP
text:
Tutorial 3 — Chemical space visualization with UMAP


Code
from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from umap import UMAP

# Load a small dataset (replace with your own SDF or CSV)
smiles_data = [
    ("Aspirin",      "CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O",     "NSAID"),
    ("Ibuprofen",    "CC(C)Cc1ccc(cc1)C(C)C(=O)O","NSAID"),
    ("Morphine",     "OC1=CC=C2CC3N(...)C2=C1",    "Opioid"),
    ("Caffeine",     "Cn1cnc2c1c(=O)n(C)c(=O)n2C","Stimulant"),
    ("Dopamine",     "NCCc1ccc(O)c(O)c1",           "Neurotransmitter"),
]

mols  = [(n, Chem.MolFromSmiles(s), c) for n, s, c in smiles_data if Chem.MolFromSmiles(s)]
fps   = np.array([AllChem.GetMorganFingerprintAsBitVect(m, 2, 1024) for _, m, _ in mols])
names = [n for n, _, _ in mols]
cats  = [c for _, _, c in mols]

coords = UMAP(n_components=2, random_state=42).fit_transform(fps)

colors = {"NSAID": "#1a5276", "Opioid": "#148f77",
          "Stimulant": "#d35400", "Neurotransmitter": "#8e44ad"}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
for cat in set(cats):
    mask = [c == cat for c in cats]
    ax.scatter(coords[mask, 0], coords[mask, 1],
               label=cat, color=colors[cat], s=80, alpha=0.8)
    for i, (x, y) in enumerate(coords[mask]):
        ax.annotate([n for n, c in zip(names, cats) if c == cat][i],
                    (x, y), fontsize=8, ha='right')

ax.set_title("Chemical space — UMAP of Morgan fingerprints")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("chemical_space_umap.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()

title: Cheminformatics Tutorials
text:
Tutorial 4 — SMARTS substructure search

from rdkit import Chem

# Define functional group SMARTS patterns
patterns = {
    "Carboxylic acid":  "[CX3](=O)[OX2H1]",
    "Ester":            "[CX3](=O)[OX2][CX4]",
    "Amine (primary)":  "[NX3;H2]",
    "Hydroxyl":         "[OX2H]",
    "Aromatic ring":    "c1ccccc1",
    "Amide":            "[CX3](=O)[NX3]",
}

test_mols = {
    "Aspirin":      "CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O",
    "Paracetamol":  "CC(=O)Nc1ccc(O)cc1",
    "Glycine":      "NCC(=O)O",
    "Caffeine":     "Cn1cnc2c1c(=O)n(C)c(=O)n2C",
}

print(f"{'Molecule':<15}", end="")
for pat_name in patterns:
    print(f"{pat_name[:14]:<16}", end="")
print()
print("-" * 110)

for mol_name, smi in test_mols.items():
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi)
    print(f"{mol_name:<15}", end="")
    for pat_name, smarts in patterns.items():
        pat = Chem.MolFromSmarts(smarts)
        hit = "YES" if mol.HasSubstructMatch(pat) else "no"
        print(f"{hit:<16}", end="")
    print()

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Aspirin        YES             no              no              YES             YES             no              
Paracetamol    no              no              no              YES             YES             YES             
Glycine        YES             no              YES             YES             no              no              
Caffeine       no              no              no              no              no              no

title: Agentic AI Tutorials
section: Tutorial 1 — A RAG pipeline over scientific literature
text:
Tutorial 1 — A RAG pipeline over scientific literature
Build a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system that answers drug-target questions by searching a corpus of your own papers and PubMed abstracts.


Code
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_community.embeddings import HuggingFaceEmbeddings
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA
from langchain.schema import Document
import requests, xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

# ── Step 1: Fetch PubMed abstracts ─────────────────────────────────────────
def fetch_pubmed(query: str, max_results: int = 20) -> list[Document]:
    base = "https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/"
    ids  = requests.get(f"{base}esearch.fcgi",
                        params={"db":"pubmed","term":query,
                                "retmax":max_results,"retmode":"json"}).json()
    pmids = ids["esearchresult"]["idlist"]
    xml   = requests.get(f"{base}efetch.fcgi",
                         params={"db":"pubmed","id":",".join(pmids),
                                 "rettype":"abstract","retmode":"xml"}).text
    root  = ET.fromstring(xml)
    docs  = []
    for art in root.iter("PubmedArticle"):
        title    = "".join(t.itertext() for t in art.iter("ArticleTitle"))
        abstract = "".join(t.itertext() for t in art.iter("AbstractText"))
        pmid     = art.findtext(".//PMID", "")
        if abstract:
            docs.append(Document(
                page_content=f"{title}\n\n{abstract}",
                metadata={"pmid": pmid, "title": title}
            ))
    return docs

# ── Step 2: Embed and index ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
docs      = fetch_pubmed("SILCS drug discovery protein-ligand binding")
embedder  = HuggingFaceEmbeddings(model_name="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
vectordb  = Chroma.from_documents(docs, embedder, persist_directory="./pubmed_db")

# ── Step 3: RAG QA chain ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514", temperature=0)
qa  = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(
    llm=llm,
    retriever=vectordb.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 4}),
    return_source_documents=True,
)

answer = qa.invoke("What is the SILCS method and how is it used in virtual screening?")
print(answer["result"])
print("\n--- Sources ---")
for doc in answer["source_documents"]:
    print(f"PMID {doc.metadata['pmid']}: {doc.metadata['title'][:80]}")

title: Agentic AI Tutorials
section: Tutorial 2 — A LangGraph agent that queries ChEMBL and PubChem
text:
Tutorial 2 — A LangGraph agent that queries ChEMBL and PubChem
Build a multi-step agent that takes a compound name, finds its structure, retrieves bioactivity data from ChEMBL, and summarizes findings.


Code
import requests
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
import operator

# ── Define tools ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@tool
def get_smiles_from_name(compound_name: str) -> str:
    """Get canonical SMILES for a compound name using PubChem."""
    url = f"https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/rest/pug/compound/name/{compound_name}/property/CanonicalSMILES/JSON"
    r = requests.get(url)
    if r.ok:
        return r.json()["PropertyTable"]["Properties"][0]["CanonicalSMILES"]
    return f"Could not find SMILES for {compound_name}"

@tool
def get_chembl_bioactivity(compound_name: str, target_keyword: str = "hERG") -> str:
    """Retrieve bioactivity data from ChEMBL for a compound against a target."""
    search_url = f"https://www.ebi.ac.uk/chembl/api/data/activity.json?molecule_pref_name__icontains={compound_name}&target_pref_name__icontains={target_keyword}&limit=5"
    r = requests.get(search_url)
    if r.ok:
        activities = r.json().get("activities", [])
        if not activities:
            return f"No ChEMBL activity found for {compound_name} vs {target_keyword}"
        lines = []
        for act in activities[:5]:
            lines.append(f"  IC50={act.get('standard_value')} {act.get('standard_units')} | assay={act.get('assay_description','N/A')[:60]}")
        return "\n".join(lines)
    return "ChEMBL API error"

# ── Agent state ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class AgentState(TypedDict):
    compound:    str
    smiles:      str
    bioactivity: str
    summary:     str
    messages:    Annotated[list, operator.add]

# ── Node functions ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
llm   = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514", temperature=0)
tools = [get_smiles_from_name, get_chembl_bioactivity]

def fetch_structure(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    smiles = get_smiles_from_name.invoke(state["compound"])
    return {"smiles": smiles}

def fetch_bioactivity(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    data = get_chembl_bioactivity.invoke({"compound_name": state["compound"]})
    return {"bioactivity": data}

def summarize(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    prompt = f"""Compound: {state['compound']}
SMILES: {state['smiles']}
ChEMBL hERG bioactivity:
{state['bioactivity']}

Write a 3-sentence summary for a medicinal chemist."""
    summary = llm.invoke(prompt).content
    return {"summary": summary}

# ── Build graph ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
graph = StateGraph(AgentState)
graph.add_node("fetch_structure",  fetch_structure)
graph.add_node("fetch_bioactivity", fetch_bioactivity)
graph.add_node("summarize",        summarize)

graph.set_entry_point("fetch_structure")
graph.add_edge("fetch_structure",   "fetch_bioactivity")
graph.add_edge("fetch_bioactivity", "summarize")
graph.add_edge("summarize",         END)

app    = graph.compile()
result = app.invoke({"compound": "terfenadine", "messages": []})
print(result["summary"])

def mergeAttachPointsToAtoms(mol,propName='attachPt'):
    if type(mol) == type('foo'):
        mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(mol)
        returnSmiles = True
    else:
        returnSmiles = False
    rwm = Chem.RWMol(mol)
    # start by tagging all the neighbors:
    toRemove = []
    for at in rwm.GetAtoms():
        if at.GetAtomicNum()==0 and at.GetDegree()==1:
            nbr = at.GetNeighbors()[0]
            if at.GetIsotope():
                iso = str(at.GetIsotope())
            else:
                iso = ''
            nbr.SetProp('attachPt',f'[{iso}*]')
            if nbr.GetIsAromatic() and nbr.GetAtomicNum() != 6:
                nbr.SetNoImplicit(True)
                nbr.SetNumExplicitHs(1)
            toRemove.append(at.GetIdx())
    # now remove the atoms
    for idx in sorted(toRemove,reverse=True):
        rwm.RemoveAtom(idx)
    if not returnSmiles:
        return rwm.GetMol()
    else:
        return Chem.MolToCXSmiles(rwm)

# do a viz where we only show nodes with at least 5 atoms:
keepNodes = set()
for i,smi in enumerate(net.nodes):
    if(Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi,sanitize=False).GetNumAtoms()<=4):
        continue
    smi = mergeAttachPointsToAtoms(smi)
    print(smi)
    keepNodes.add(i)
    qsmi = parse.quote(smi)
    imgurl = f"https://mol-renderer2-dev.t5ix.io/to_img/mol.png?smiles={qsmi}&w=200&h=150"
    g.add_node(i,shape="image",label=' ',
               image =imgurl, title=f'<img src="{imgurl}"/>', size=30*net.counts[i]/maxCount)
for e in net.edges:
    if e.beginIdx in keepNodes and e.endIdx in keepNodes:
        g.add_edge(e.beginIdx,e.endIdx,label=str(e.type))

Planning

If not provided or if target language definition is ambiguous, ask for examples of valid strings to be parsed
Before developing the pyparsing expressions, define a Backus-Naur Form definition and save this in docs/grammar.md. Update this document as changes are made in the parser.

Implementing

Import pyparsing using import pyparsing as pp, and use that for all pyparsing references.

If referencing names from pyparsing.common, follow the pyparsing import with “ppc = pp.common” and use ppc as the namespace to access pyparsing.common.
If referencing names from pyparsing.unicode, follow the pyparsing import with “ppu = pp.unicode” and use ppu as the namespace to access pyparsing.unicode.

When writing parsers that contain recursive elements (using Forward() or infix_notation()), immediately enable packrat parsing for performance: pp.ParserElement.enable_packrat() (call this right after importing pyparsing). See https://pyparsing-docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/HowToUsePyparsing.html.

For recursive grammars, define placeholders with pp.Forward() and assign later using the <<= operator; give Forwards meaningful names with set_name() to improve errors.

Use PEP8 method and argument names in the pyparsing API (parse_string, not parseString).
Do not include expressions for matching whitespace in the grammar. Pyparsing skips whitespace by default.
For line-oriented grammars where newlines are significant, set skippable whitespace to just spaces/tabs early: pp.ParserElement.set_default_whitespace_chars(" \t"), and define NL = pp.LineEnd().suppress() to handle line ends explicitly.
Prefer operator forms for readability: use +, |, ^, ~, etc., instead of explicit And/MatchFirst/Or/Not classes (see Usage notes in https://pyparsing-docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/HowToUsePyparsing.html).
Use set_name() on all major grammar elements to support railroad diagramming and better error/debug output.
The grammar should be independently testable, without pulling in separate modules for data structures, evaluation, or command execution.
Use results names for robust access to parsed data fields; results names should be valid Python identifiers to support attribute-style access on returned ParseResults.

Results names should take the place of numeric indexing into parsed results in most places.
Define results names using call format not set_results_name(), example: full_name = Word(alphas)("first_name") + Word(alphas)("last_name")
If adding results name to an expression that is contains one more sub-expressions with results names, the expression must be inclused in a Group.

Prefer Keyword over Literal for reserved words to avoid partial matches (e.g., Keyword("for") will not match the leading “for” in “format”).

Use pp.CaselessKeyword/pp.CaselessLiteral when keywords should match regardless of case.

When the full input must be consumed, call parse_string with parse_all=True.
If the grammar must handle comments, define an expression for them and use the ignore() method to skip them.

Prefer built-ins like pp.cpp_style_comment and pp.python_style_comment for common comment syntaxes.

Use pyparsing Group to organize sub-expressions. Groups are also important for preserving results names when a sub-expression is used in a OneOrMore or ZeroOrMore expression.
Suppress punctuation tokens to keep results clean; a convenient pattern is LBRACK, RBRACK, LBRACE, RBRACE, COLON = pp.Suppress.using_each("[]{}:").
For comma-separated sequences, prefer pp.DelimitedList(...); wrap with pp.Optional(...) to allow empty lists or objects where appropriate.
For helper sub-expressions used only to build larger expressions, consider set_name(None) to keep result dumps uncluttered.
Use pyparsing Each() to define a list of elements that may occur in any order.

The ‘&’ operator is the operator form of Each and is often more readable when combining order-independent parts.

Use parse actions to do parse-time conversion of data from strings to useful data types.

Use objects defined in pyparsing.common for common types like integer, real — these already have their conversion parse actions defined.
For quoted strings, use pp.dbl_quoted_string().set_parse_action(pp.remove_quotes) to unquote automatically.
Map reserved words to Python constants per this example for parsing “true” to auto-convert to a Python True: pp.Keyword("true").set_parse_action(pp.replace_with(True)) (and similarly for false/null/etc.).
When you want native Python containers from the parse, use pp.Group(..., aslist=True) for lists and pp.Dict(..., asdict=True) for dict-like data.

Use “using_each” with a list of keywords to define keyword constants, instead of separate assignments.
Choose the appropriate matching method:

parse_string() parses from the start
search_string() searches anywhere in the text
scan_string() yields all matches with positions
transform_string() is a convenience wrapper around scan_string to apply filters or transforms defined in parse actions, to perform batch transforms or conversions of expressions within a larger body of text

For line suffixes or directives, combine lookahead and slicing helpers: pp.FollowedBy(...) with pp.rest_of_line; when reusing a base expression with a different parse action, call .copy() before applying the new action to avoid side effects.
When defining a parser to be used in a REPL:

add pyparsing Tag() elements of the form Tag("command", <command-name>) to each command definition to support model construction from parsed commands.
define model classes using dataclasses, and use the “command” attribute in the parsed results to identify which model class to create. The model classes can then be used to construct the model from the ParseResults returned by parse_string(). Define the models in a separate parser_models.py file.

If defining the grammar as part of a Parser class, only the finished grammar needs to be implemented as an instance variable.
ParseResults support “in” testing for results names. Use “in” tests for the existence of results names, not hasattr().
Avoid left recursion where possible. If you must support left-recursive grammars, enable it with pp.ParserElement.enable_left_recursion() and do not enable packrat at the same time (these modes are incompatible).
Use pp.SkipTo as a skipping expression to skip over arbitrary content.

For example, pp.SkipTo(pp.LineEnd()) will skip over all content until the end of the line; add a stop_on argument to SkipTo to stop skipping when a particular string is matched.
Use ... in place of simple SkipTo(expression)

Testing

Use the pyparsing ParserElement.run_tests method to run mini validation tests.

Pass a single multiline string to run_tests to test the parser on multiple test input strings, each line is a separate test.
You can add comments starting with “#” within the string passed to run_tests to document the individual test cases.
To pass test input strings that span multiple lines, pass the test input strings as a list of strings.
Pass parse_all=True to run_tests to test that the entire input is consumed.

When generating unit tests for the parser:

generate tests that include presence and absence of optional elements
use the methods in the mixin class pyparsing.testing.TestParseResultsAsserts to easily define expression, test input string, and expected results
do not generate tests for invalid data

Debugging

If troubleshooting parse actions, use pyparsing’s trace_parse_action decorator to echo arguments and return value
During development, call pp.autoname_elements() to auto-assign names to unnamed expressions to improve dump() and error messages.
Sub-expressions can be tested in isolation using ParserElement.matches()
When defined out of order, Literals can mistakenly match fragments: Literal("for") will match the leading “for” in “format”. Can be corrected by using Keyword instead of Literal.
Dump the parsed results using ParseResults.dump(), ParseResults.pprint(), or repr(ParseResults).

title: Code & GitHub
text:
Toxicogenomics & bioinformatics

🧫 rnaseq-tox-pipeline
End-to-end RNA-Seq analysis pipeline for toxicogenomics studies. Covers QC, alignment, DESeq2 differential expression, pathway analysis, and in vitro–in vivo correlation. Validated on rat, guinea pig, and human hepatocyte data.
Key scripts: run_pipeline.sh · deseq2_analysis.R · pathway_enrichment.R · ivive_correlation.py
Tags: rna-seq toxicogenomics bioinformatics deseq2
View on GitHub →



📊 mea-neurotox
Analysis of Multi-Electrode Array (MEA) assay data for computational neurotoxicity profiling. Implements dose-response modeling via tcplfit2 and chemical-activity similarity analysis.
Key scripts: mea_preprocess.py · dose_response.R · activity_similarity.py · fingerprint_mea_correlation.py
Tags: neurotoxicity mea dose-response high-throughput
View on GitHub →

title: Code & GitHub
text:
Machine learning & AI

🤖 agentic-ai-drugdiscovery
Agentic AI workflows for drug discovery tasks using LangGraph, LangChain, and RAG. Includes tool-calling agents that query PubChem, ChEMBL, and UniProt, plus a RAG pipeline over scientific literature.
Key scripts: agent_drugquery.py · rag_pipeline.py · chembl_tool.py · pubchem_tool.py
Tags: agentic-ai langgraph rag llm drug-discovery
View on GitHub →



🔬 ml-compound-classifier
ML pipeline for classifying active/inactive compounds against neurotoxicity-relevant GPCR targets. Compares traditional ML (RF, XGBoost), DNNs, GNNs, and transformer-based LLMs using DeepChem and PyTorch.
Key scripts: featurize_compounds.py · train_rf_xgb.py · train_gnn.py · train_transformer.py · benchmark.py
Tags: machine-learning gnn transformer cheminformatics deepchem
View on GitHub →



🧪 generative-mol-design
Generative AI for novel molecule design — variational autoencoders, graph-based generative models, and LLM-guided molecular generation. Supports drug repurposing workflows.
Key scripts: vae_train.py · graph_gen.py · llm_mol_gen.py · admet_filter.py
Tags: generative-ai molecule-generation drug-repurposing vae

from p_con import p_con
pco = p_con(“P43088”) pco.verbous = True
pco.step_0_get_chembl_data() # Download Compounds for P43088 from ChEMBL (or pco.load_mols(“sdf-file.sdf.gz”))
pco.step_1_keeplargestfrag() # remove small Fragments from compounds
pco.step_2_remove_dupl() # remove duplicate-Entries
pco.step_3_merge_IC50() # merge IC50 from Entries with same canonical smiles into one compound
pco.step_4_set_TL(4000,ic50_tag=“value”) # set TrafficLights, value > 4000nm: 0, else 1
pco.step_5_remove_descriptors() # remove Descriptors from compounds
pco.step_6_calc_descriptors() # calculate new Descriptors which are used to create prediction-models
pco.step_7_train_models() # train up to 10 models
pco.save_model_info(“model_info.csv”,mode=“csv”) # create csv with data for each model pco.save_model_info(“model_info.html”,mode=“html”) # create html -#-
for i in range(len(pco.model)): pco.save_model(“model_%d.pkl” % i,i)
for i in range(len(pco.model)): act,inact = pco.predict(i) print “Model %d active: %d: %d” % (i,act,inact)

About me
I’m a computational scientist with 7+ years of interdisciplinary experience spanning computational chemistry, biology, and toxicology. My work sits at the intersection of AI/ML, drug discovery, and translational safety research.
Most recently I was a Computational Research Scientist II at BHSAI / Henry M. Jackson Foundation (Frederick, MD), working on DoD, DTRA, NIH, and AFRL funded projects. Before that I was a postdoctoral researcher in Dr. Alex MacKerell’s CADD Center at the University of Maryland, Baltimore, where I developed and applied the SILCS technology for protein-ligand binding and drug design.
I’m currently open to new opportunities in computational drug discovery, AI/ML for life sciences, or research scientist roles. Get in touch →

section: Tutorial 1 — Classical ML baseline for molecular property prediction
text:
Tutorial 1 — Classical ML baseline for molecular property prediction
Before reaching for deep learning, always establish a strong classical ML baseline.

from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem, Descriptors, rdMolDescriptors
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score

def get_features(smiles: str) -> np.ndarray:
    """Morgan fingerprint + physicochemical descriptors."""
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)
    if mol is None:
        return None
    fp   = AllChem.GetMorganFingerprintAsBitVect(mol, radius=2, nBits=1024)
    desc = [
        Descriptors.ExactMolWt(mol),
        Descriptors.MolLogP(mol),
        Descriptors.TPSA(mol),
        rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumHBD(mol),
        rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumHBA(mol),
        rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumRotatableBonds(mol),
        rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumAromaticRings(mol),
    ]
    return np.concatenate([list(fp), desc])

# Simulated hERG dataset (replace with real data from ChEMBL)
np.random.seed(42)
smiles_active   = ["CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O"] * 50   # placeholder
smiles_inactive = ["Cn1cnc2c1c(=O)n(C)c(=O)n2C"] * 50

X = np.array([get_features(s) for s in smiles_active + smiles_inactive])
y = np.array([1]*50 + [0]*50)

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for name, clf in [("Random Forest",   RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)),
                  ("Gradient Boost",  GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42))]:
    scores = cross_val_score(clf, X_scaled, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc")
    print(f"{name:20s}  AUC = {scores.mean():.3f} ± {scores.std():.3f}")

Random Forest         AUC = 1.000 ± 0.000
Gradient Boost        AUC = 1.000 ± 0.000

title: ML & Multimodal Tutorials
section: Tutorial 2 — Graph Neural Network for molecular property prediction
text:
Tutorial 2 — Graph Neural Network for molecular property prediction
Molecules are graphs — GNNs directly encode atoms (nodes) and bonds (edges).


Code
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch_geometric.nn import GCNConv, global_mean_pool
from torch_geometric.data import Data, DataLoader
from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem

def mol_to_graph(smiles: str, label: float) -> Data:
    """Convert SMILES to PyG graph."""
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)
    if mol is None:
        return None

    # Node features: atomic number, degree, formal charge, aromaticity
    x = []
    for atom in mol.GetAtoms():
        x.append([
            atom.GetAtomicNum(),
            atom.GetDegree(),
            atom.GetFormalCharge(),
            int(atom.GetIsAromatic()),
            atom.GetTotalNumHs(),
        ])
    x = torch.tensor(x, dtype=torch.float)

    # Edge indices (bonds)
    edge_index = []
    for bond in mol.GetBonds():
        i, j = bond.GetBeginAtomIdx(), bond.GetEndAtomIdx()
        edge_index += [[i, j], [j, i]]
    edge_index = torch.tensor(edge_index, dtype=torch.long).t().contiguous()

    return Data(x=x, edge_index=edge_index, y=torch.tensor([label], dtype=torch.float))

class MolGCN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels=5, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = GCNConv(in_channels, hidden)
        self.conv2 = GCNConv(hidden, hidden)
        self.conv3 = GCNConv(hidden, hidden)
        self.fc    = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index, batch):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))
        x = F.relu(self.conv2(x, edge_index))
        x = F.relu(self.conv3(x, edge_index))
        x = global_mean_pool(x, batch)   # graph-level embedding
        return torch.sigmoid(self.fc(x)).squeeze()

# Training loop
model     = MolGCN()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
criterion = nn.BCELoss()

# (Replace with real DataLoader from your dataset)
for epoch in range(50):
    model.train()
    for batch in train_loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        pred = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)
        loss = criterion(pred, batch.y)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
    if epoch % 10 == 0:
        print(f"Epoch {epoch:3d}  loss={loss.item():.4f}")

title: ML & Multimodal Tutorials
text:
Tutorial 3 — Transfer learning with ChemBERTa
Use a pre-trained molecular transformer (ChemBERTa) and fine-tune it on your toxicity dataset.


Code
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader

MODEL_NAME = "seyonec/ChemBERTa-zinc-base-v1"
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)
model      = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME, num_labels=2)

class SmilesDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, smiles_list, labels, tokenizer, max_len=128):
        self.encodings = tokenizer(smiles_list, truncation=True,
                                   padding=True, max_length=max_len,
                                   return_tensors="pt")
        self.labels = torch.tensor(labels, dtype=torch.long)

    def __len__(self):  return len(self.labels)
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return {k: v[idx] for k, v in self.encodings.items()}, self.labels[idx]

# Fine-tuning
smiles_train = ["CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O", "Cn1cnc2c1c(=O)n(C)c(=O)n2C"]
labels_train = [1, 0]

dataset    = SmilesDataset(smiles_train, labels_train, tokenizer)
loader     = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=8, shuffle=True)
optimizer  = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-5)

model.train()
for epoch in range(3):
    for batch, labels in loader:
        outputs = model(**batch, labels=labels)
        loss    = outputs.loss
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        optimizer.zero_grad()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}  loss={loss.item():.4f}")

model.save_pretrained("chemberta_toxicity_finetuned")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("chemberta_toxicity_finetuned")

section: Tutorial 1 — Molecular fingerprints and similarity search
text:
Tutorial 1 — Molecular fingerprints and similarity search
A hands-on introduction to molecular representation and similarity searching using RDKit — the foundation of cheminformatics and CADD.

from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem, DataStructs, Draw
from rdkit.Chem import rdMolDescriptors
import pandas as pd

# Example: compare known hERG blockers vs non-blockers
smiles_dict = {
    "Terfenadine (blocker)":  "OC(c1ccc(C(c2ccccc2)(c2ccccc2)O)cc1)CCCN1CCC(CC1)C(O)(c1ccccc1)c1ccccc1",
    "Cisapride (blocker)":    "CCOC(=O)c1cc2cc(OC)c(OC)cc2[nH]1",
    "Metformin (non-blocker)":"CN(C)C(=N)NC(=N)N",
    "Caffeine (non-blocker)": "Cn1cnc2c1c(=O)n(C)c(=O)n2C",
}

mols = {name: Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi) for name, smi in smiles_dict.items()}
fps  = {name: AllChem.GetMorganFingerprintAsBitVect(mol, radius=2, nBits=2048)
        for name, mol in mols.items() if mol}

# Tanimoto similarity matrix
rows = []
for n1, fp1 in fps.items():
    for n2, fp2 in fps.items():
        rows.append({
            "Mol A": n1.split(" ")[0],
            "Mol B": n2.split(" ")[0],
            "Tanimoto": round(DataStructs.TanimotoSimilarity(fp1, fp2), 3)
        })

sim_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
pivot  = sim_df.pivot(index="Mol A", columns="Mol B", values="Tanimoto")
print(pivot.to_string())

Mol B        Caffeine  Cisapride  Metformin  Terfenadine
Mol A                                                   
Caffeine        1.000      0.074      0.024        0.063
Cisapride       0.074      1.000      0.042        0.056
Metformin       0.024      0.042      1.000        0.017
Terfenadine     0.063      0.056      0.017        1.000


Key observations: - Terfenadine and Cisapride (both hERG blockers) show low Tanimoto similarity — toxicity isn’t structurally conserved - This motivates feature-based ML over pure similarity search for safety prediction - Morgan fingerprints (ECFP) encode local chemical environments at radius=2 (ECFP4)

title: Drug Design & CADD Tutorials
section: Tutorial 2 — Lipinski’s Rule of Five and ADMET profiling
text:
Tutorial 2 — Lipinski’s Rule of Five and ADMET profiling
Screen a compound library for drug-likeness before committing to expensive docking runs.


Code
from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import Descriptors, rdMolDescriptors
import pandas as pd

def compute_ro5(smiles: str, name: str = "") -> dict:
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)
    if mol is None:
        return {}
    mw   = Descriptors.ExactMolWt(mol)
    logp = Descriptors.MolLogP(mol)
    hbd  = rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumHBD(mol)
    hba  = rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumHBA(mol)
    tpsa = Descriptors.TPSA(mol)
    rot  = rdMolDescriptors.CalcNumRotatableBonds(mol)
    pass_ro5 = (mw <= 500) and (logp <= 5) and (hbd <= 5) and (hba <= 10)
    return {
        "Name": name, "MW": round(mw, 1), "LogP": round(logp, 2),
        "HBD": hbd, "HBA": hba, "TPSA": round(tpsa, 1),
        "RotBonds": rot, "Pass Ro5": "✅" if pass_ro5 else "❌"
    }

# Example library
library = {
    "Aspirin":       "CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O",
    "Ibuprofen":     "CC(C)Cc1ccc(cc1)C(C)C(=O)O",
    "Atorvastatin":  "CC(C)c1c(C(=O)Nc2ccccc2)c(-c2ccccc2)c(-c2ccc(F)cc2)n1CCC(O)CC(O)CC(=O)O",
    "Paclitaxel":    "O=C(Nc1ccccc1)c1ccccc1OC(=O)[C@@H]2[C@@H](OC(C)(C)C(=O)O)CC[C@@]3(O)C(=O)[C@H](OC(=O)c4ccccc4)[C@@H](CC[C@H]2OC(C)=O)O3",
    "Cyclosporin A": "CCC1NC(=O)C(C(O)C(C)CC=CC)N(C)C(=O)C(C(C)CC)N(C)C(=O)C(CC(C)C)N(C)C(=O)C(CC(C)C)N(C)C(=O)C(C)N(C)C(=O)C(CC(C)C)N(C)C(=O)C(CC(C)C)N(C)C(=O)C(C)N(C)C(=O)CN(C)C1=O",
}

results = pd.DataFrame([compute_ro5(smi, name) for name, smi in library.items()])
print(results.to_string(index=False))


         Name     MW  LogP  HBD  HBA  TPSA  RotBonds Pass Ro5
      Aspirin  180.0  1.31    1    3  63.6         2        ✅
    Ibuprofen  206.1  3.07    1    1  37.3         4        ✅
 Atorvastatin  558.3  6.31    4    5 111.8        12        ❌
   Paclitaxel  717.2  4.10    3   12 201.1        10        ❌
Cyclosporin A 1144.8  4.20    2   11 232.1        15        ❌



Paclitaxel and Cyclosporin A violate Ro5 — yet both are approved drugs.
Ro5 is a guideline for oral drugs; large natural-product scaffolds often reach their targets by other routes.

title: Drug Design & CADD Tutorials
section: Tutorial 3 — Virtual screening with AutoDock Vina (Python wrapper)
text:
Tutorial 3 — Virtual screening with AutoDock Vina (Python wrapper)
Set up a basic docking experiment against a receptor from the PDB.


Code
import subprocess, os
from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem

def prepare_ligand(smiles: str, out_pdbqt: str):
    """Convert SMILES → 3D → PDBQT for Vina."""
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)
    mol = Chem.AddHs(mol)
    AllChem.EmbedMolecule(mol, AllChem.ETKDGv3())
    AllChem.MMFFOptimizeMolecule(mol)
    Chem.MolToMolFile(mol, out_pdbqt.replace(".pdbqt", ".mol"))
    # Convert with Open Babel (must be installed)
    subprocess.run(["obabel", out_pdbqt.replace(".pdbqt", ".mol"),
                    "-O", out_pdbqt, "--gen3d"], check=True)

def run_vina(receptor_pdbqt: str, ligand_pdbqt: str,
             center: tuple, box_size: tuple = (20, 20, 20),
             exhaustiveness: int = 8) -> float:
    """Run Vina and return best docking score (kcal/mol)."""
    cmd = [
        "vina",
        "--receptor", receptor_pdbqt,
        "--ligand",   ligand_pdbqt,
        "--center_x", str(center[0]),
        "--center_y", str(center[1]),
        "--center_z", str(center[2]),
        "--size_x",   str(box_size[0]),
        "--size_y",   str(box_size[1]),
        "--size_z",   str(box_size[2]),
        "--exhaustiveness", str(exhaustiveness),
        "--out", ligand_pdbqt.replace(".pdbqt", "_out.pdbqt"),
    ]
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    for line in result.stdout.split("\n"):
        if line.strip().startswith("1"):
            return float(line.split()[1])
    return 0.0

# Example: dock a small library against SARS-CoV-2 main protease (PDB: 6LU7)
# center_x/y/z from known active site
library_smiles = {
    "Nirmatrelvir": "CC1(C2CC2CN1C(=O)C(NC(=O)C3CC3NC(=O)C(C#N)=C)C(C)(C)C)F",
    "GC-376":       "CC(C)(C)OC(=O)NC1CC(=O)N2CCCC12",
    "Ebselen":      "O=C1OC(c2ccccc21)=Nc1ccccc1[Se]1",
}

results = {}
for name, smi in library_smiles.items():
    lig_pdbqt = f"/tmp/{name.replace(' ','_')}.pdbqt"
    prepare_ligand(smi, lig_pdbqt)
    score = run_vina("6lu7_receptor.pdbqt", lig_pdbqt,
                     center=(-10.4, 13.1, 59.1))
    results[name] = score
    print(f"{name:25s}  {score:6.2f} kcal/mol")



This tutorial requires AutoDock Vina and Open Babel installed.
See the GitHub repo for a ready-to-run Docker container.

text:
Tutorial 1 — RNA-Seq QC and preprocessing
A well-structured RNA-Seq pipeline starts with quality checks before any analysis.


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Simulate a count matrix (genes x samples)
np.random.seed(42)
n_genes, n_samples = 500, 6
gene_names   = [f"Gene_{i:04d}" for i in range(n_genes)]
sample_names = ["Control_1","Control_2","Control_3","Treated_1","Treated_2","Treated_3"]

# Simulate counts: treated samples have ~20% genes upregulated
counts = np.random.negative_binomial(5, 0.3, size=(n_genes, n_samples))
counts[50:100, 3:] = (counts[50:100, 3:] * 4).astype(int)  # upregulated
counts[100:130, 3:] = (counts[100:130, 3:] * 0.2).astype(int)  # downregulated

count_df = pd.DataFrame(counts, index=gene_names, columns=sample_names)

# Basic QC: library sizes
lib_sizes = count_df.sum()
print("Library sizes (total counts per sample):")
for s, n in lib_sizes.items():
    print(f"  {s}: {n:,}")

# Flag low-count genes
low_count = (count_df < 10).all(axis=1).sum()
print(f"\nGenes with counts < 10 in ALL samples: {low_count} ({low_count/n_genes*100:.1f}%) — will be filtered")

# After filtering
filtered = count_df[count_df.sum(axis=1) >= 10]
print(f"Genes remaining after filter: {len(filtered)}")


Library sizes (total counts per sample):
  Control_1: 5,604
  Control_2: 6,174
  Control_3: 5,623
  Treated_1: 7,154
  Treated_2: 7,672
  Treated_3: 7,499

title: Bioinformatics & RNA-Seq Tutorials
text:
Tutorial 2 — Differential expression with PyDESeq2


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from pydeseq2.dds import DeseqDataSet
from pydeseq2.ds import DeseqStats

# Load your count matrix (genes as rows, samples as columns)
counts = pd.read_csv("counts.csv", index_col=0)

# Sample metadata
metadata = pd.DataFrame({
    "condition": ["control","control","control","treated","treated","treated"]
}, index=counts.columns)

# Build DESeq2 dataset
dds = DeseqDataSet(
    counts=counts.T,           # PyDESeq2 expects samples as rows
    metadata=metadata,
    design_factors="condition",
    refit_cooks=True,
)
dds.deseq2()

# Run Wald test
stat_res = DeseqStats(dds, contrast=["condition", "treated", "control"])
stat_res.summary()

results = stat_res.results_df
results = results.dropna().sort_values("padj")

# Filter significant DEGs
sig = results[(results["padj"] < 0.05) & (results["log2FoldChange"].abs() > 1)]
print(f"Significant DEGs (padj<0.05, |LFC|>1): {len(sig)}")
print(f"  Upregulated:   {(sig['log2FoldChange'] > 0).sum()}")
print(f"  Downregulated: {(sig['log2FoldChange'] < 0).sum()}")
print(sig.head(10)[["baseMean","log2FoldChange","pvalue","padj"]])

title: Bioinformatics & RNA-Seq Tutorials
text:
Tutorial 3 — Volcano plot visualization


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

# Simulate DESeq2-like results
np.random.seed(0)
n = 1000
log2fc = np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)
pval   = np.random.uniform(0, 1, n)
# Make some genes truly significant
log2fc[50:80]  =  np.random.uniform(2, 5, 30)
pval[50:80]    =  np.random.uniform(1e-10, 1e-4, 30)
log2fc[80:100] = -np.random.uniform(2, 5, 20)
pval[80:100]   =  np.random.uniform(1e-8, 1e-3, 20)

neg_log10_p = -np.log10(pval + 1e-300)
padj_thresh = 0.05
lfc_thresh  = 1.0

up   = (log2fc >  lfc_thresh) & (pval < padj_thresh)
down = (log2fc < -lfc_thresh) & (pval < padj_thresh)
ns   = ~up & ~down

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.scatter(log2fc[ns],   neg_log10_p[ns],   c="#b0b0b0", s=12, alpha=0.5, label=f"NS ({ns.sum()})")
ax.scatter(log2fc[up],   neg_log10_p[up],   c="#c0392b", s=20, alpha=0.8, label=f"Up ({up.sum()})")
ax.scatter(log2fc[down], neg_log10_p[down], c="#2980b9", s=20, alpha=0.8, label=f"Down ({down.sum()})")

ax.axhline(-np.log10(padj_thresh), color="black", linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8)
ax.axvline( lfc_thresh,  color="gray", linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8)
ax.axvline(-lfc_thresh,  color="gray", linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8)

ax.set_xlabel("log2 Fold Change", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("-log10(p-value)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Volcano plot — Treated vs Control", fontsize=13)
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("volcano_plot.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()

title: Bioinformatics & RNA-Seq Tutorials
text:
Tutorial 4 — In vitro–in vivo correlation (IVIVE)
A key part of translational toxicology — correlating gene expression changes between animal models and human cell systems.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# Simulate log2FC values for the same genes in two systems
np.random.seed(7)
n_genes = 200
# Good IVIVE: most genes correlate, some scatter
invivo_lfc  = np.random.normal(0, 2, n_genes)
invitro_lfc = invivo_lfc * 0.75 + np.random.normal(0, 0.8, n_genes)

r, pval = stats.pearsonr(invivo_lfc, invitro_lfc)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
ax.scatter(invivo_lfc, invitro_lfc, alpha=0.5, s=20, color="#1a5276")

# Regression line
m, b = np.polyfit(invivo_lfc, invitro_lfc, 1)
x_line = np.linspace(invivo_lfc.min(), invivo_lfc.max(), 100)
ax.plot(x_line, m * x_line + b, color="#d35400", linewidth=1.5)

ax.set_xlabel("In vivo log2FC (rat)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("In vitro log2FC (human hepatocytes)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title(f"IVIVE correlation  r = {r:.3f}, p = {pval:.2e}", fontsize=12)
ax.axhline(0, color="gray", linewidth=0.5)
ax.axvline(0, color="gray", linewidth=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("ivive_correlation.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()
